# hilavitkutin — Architecture Principles

<!-- BEGIN: shared cross-ecosystem principles (synced from clause-dev/.shared/principles-fragment.md — do not edit in place) -->

## No heap. Ever.

No ad-hoc allocations. Allocation is scheduler-managed, declared at
compile time, never runtime-fabricated. `#![no_std]` + `#![no_alloc]`
aren't crate attributes first — they're the universe. `Vec`, `Box`,
`Rc`, `Arc`, `HashMap`, `String`, `format!` don't exist here.

If an API feels like it wants a runtime-growable container, the shape
is wrong: reshape toward a trait-bound emitter / iterator / bounded
store. The heap is not temporarily available; there is no "just for
this moment".

## No bare primitives. Anywhere.

`u8` through `u128`, `i8` through `i128`, `f32`, `f64`, `usize`,
`isize`, `bool`, `Option`, `Result`, `String`, non-static `&str` —
**they do not exist in this stack.** Not in public signatures, not
in private fields, not in internal `let` bindings, not in cast
expressions, not in literal suffixes, not in type aliases, not in
`const`/`static` declarations, not in trait method parameter types,
not in `where` clauses, not in tuple-struct fields.

Substitute vocabulary lives in arvo (`UFixed` / `IFixed` /
`FastFloat` / `StrictFloat` / `USize` / `Cap` / `Bool`) and notko
(`Maybe` / `Outcome` / `Just`). hilavitkutin-str supplies `Str` for
interned string identity. The `arvo-types-only`, `no-bare-numeric`,
`no-bare-option`, `no-bare-result`, `no-bare-string`, and
`no-public-raw-field` lints scan every line of every `.rs` file and
block commits the moment a bare primitive is physically written.

If a foreign crate's trait or an unavoidable language feature forces
a specific std primitive, the first question is: *can we implement
the foreign contract on an arvo type instead?* If yes, that's the
path. If no, the crate smells like ass and we drop it. The escape
hatch `// lint:allow(<rule>) reason: ...; tracked: #N` exists only
for the genuinely unavoidable boundary, always with a tracked
remediation task.

## Think in traits, not types

Signatures take trait bounds. The concrete type is whatever the
caller brings.

**The question isn't "what type goes here"; it's "what is the contract
this position represents".** Monomorphisation makes trait-generic
signatures compile to the same code as concrete ones. Inference at
call sites means no turbofish.

Patterns throughout the stack are instances of this single mental
model: arvo's `UArith<BITS>` / `IArith<BITS>`, arvo-bits' `Bool: Try`,
hilavitkutin-api's 22 access/context/platform traits, saalis's
"no Vec in trait signatures". All the same rule.

**Decision test:** "Can I imagine two implementations with different
behavior?" If yes, it's a trait slot, not a concrete type.

## No `Vec` or `Box<[T]>` in trait signatures

Direct consequence of the two principles above. Trait return types
use `impl IntoIterator`, `AsRef<[T]>`, or associated types. Sink
positions take `&mut impl Collector<T>` / `&mut impl DiagnosticSink`
/ `&mut impl ByteEmitter`. Concrete collection types are
implementation choices, not contracts.

Collections never cross ecosystem API boundaries. They live below
the boundary, on the implementer's side. The boundary carries data
flow, not storage.

## No `dyn`, no `Box<dyn>`, no runtime dispatch

All dispatch goes through monomorphised trait bounds. `dyn Trait`
forces late binding, heap via `Box<dyn>`, and vtable layouts that
aren't stable across compilations or `dlopen` boundaries.

Where extension across an ABI boundary is required, use registered
traits + `repr(C)` descriptors (inventory pattern), not `dyn`.

## Type taxonomy

Every type anywhere in source — public or private, fn sig or field,
local or cast expression — falls into one of three categories:

| Category | When | Examples |
|----------|------|----------|
| **Trait** | Varying behavior, shared contract, extensible | arithmetic ops, sinks, platform providers |
| **Newtype** | Fixed representation, validation at construction | `UFixed<I, F, S>`, `USize`, `Bool`, `ContentHash`, strategy markers |
| **`repr(C)` enum** | Closed set, crosses ABI boundary | `Priority`, `ErrorClass` |

There is no fourth "bare primitive" category. Raw `u8`..`u128`,
`i8`..`i128`, `f32`, `f64`, `usize`, `isize`, `bool` never appear in
source; every numeric identity flows through arvo's type system.

Decision test is the same as above: if two plausible implementations
could differ, it's a trait.

If you're asking "should this enum have room for plugin extensions?"
— it shouldn't be an enum. It should be a trait.

## Sizes are const at type level

Every container's capacity lives at type level: `Mask64`, `Mask256`,
`Seq<T, N: Cap>`, `Map<K, V, N: Cap>`. No runtime resize, no runtime
grow. If a consumer needs a bigger container, they pick the bigger
type.

This is what the "no heap" principle bottoms out in at the storage
layer: capacity is a compile-time choice the consumer makes once,
not a runtime allocation the callee fabricates.

## One mechanism, not two

If two subsystems solve the same problem differently, one must go.
No parallel versioning tables, no parallel serialization paths, no
"fast path" alongside a "slow path". One mechanism per concern.

## Registered traits over `repr(C)` enums

If a concept might need extension, it's a trait + `#[register]` (or
the stack's inventory equivalent), not a `repr(C)` enum. Enums are
for truly closed sets only (`Priority`, `ErrorClass`, `FieldKind`,
`HealthStatus`).

## `Named::NAME` single source of truth

Every registrable type has `const NAME: &'static str`. The
descriptor's name field is a const pointer to it. No duplication,
no copies, no parallel naming.

## ZST markers for discrimination

Schema kinds, events, settings domains, storage locations, access
levels, HTTP methods — all ZSTs. Marker types carry compile-time
identity without runtime footprint.

## Primitive vocabulary (the stack's own)

The rule is "no bare primitives anywhere" (section above); the table
here documents WHICH stack type replaces WHICH bare primitive. It is
not a "for public API only" table. Every mapping applies everywhere
the bare primitive might otherwise appear.

| Don't | Do | Lives in |
|-------|----|----------|
| `Option<T>` | `Maybe<T>` | `notko` |
| `Result<T, E>` | `Outcome<T, E>` | `notko` |
| `Vec<T>` return | `impl IntoIterator<Item=T>` / trait-bound sink | trait at the API boundary |
| `&mut Vec<T>` param | `&mut impl Collector<T>` | hilavitkutin-api contracts |
| `HashMap<K, V>` | `&mut impl KeyValueStore<K, V>` / `Map<K, V, N>` | hilavitkutin-api |
| `String` owned | `Str` | `hilavitkutin-str` |
| `&str` non-static | `&'static str` or `Str` | as above |
| `usize` / `isize` | `USize` / `ISize` / `Cap` / domain newtype | `arvo::newtype` |
| `u8..u128` / `i8..i128` | `UFixed` / `IFixed` / `T: Boundable` / `T: NonZeroable` | `arvo` / `notko` traits |
| `f32` / `f64` | `FastFloat` / `StrictFloat` | `arvo::newtype` |
| `bool` | `Bool` | `arvo::newtype` |

Escape hatches: std trait method impls whose signatures are fixed by
the language (`fn next() -> Option<Self::Item>`, `fn partial_cmp() ->
Option<Ordering>`, `fn fmt() -> fmt::Result`). Each one carries
`// lint:allow(<rule>) reason: std trait parity; tracked: #N`.
Everywhere else, the rule stands.

`lint:allow(...)` is NOT for skeleton convenience; it's for tracked
debt with a linked task id. If the suppression isn't traceable to a
specific follow-up, the design is wrong, not the rule.

<!-- END: shared cross-ecosystem principles -->

---

*The following principles are specific to hilavitkutin.*

## no_std, no alloc

Every crate is `#![no_std]` with no allocation. Consumers provide a
`MemoryProvider` (os tier uses mmap; std tier uses std::alloc; no_os
tier requires the consumer to plug one in).

The engine never calls into the allocator at runtime. Plan-time
allocation (arena sizing, fiber layouts) happens once per pipeline
lifetime, not per frame.

## Monomorphisation is the dispatch

No `dyn`. No `TypeId`. No `std::any`. WUs are generic over their
AccessSet; the monomorphised function IS the type identity. LLVM
devirtualises the fused pipeline into straight-line code.

ExpandedLto pragma (domain 03, hilavitkutin-build) is REQUIRED for
devirtualisation.

## Pre-allocated thread pool

Threads = `min(physical_cores, parallelisable_width + 1)`. Pool
persists for pipeline lifetime. Each pool thread pinned to a
specific core when possible. No runtime spawn.

Hybrid wake: spin 128 iterations, then park. Predictive parking at
sync points: <100ns spin, 100ns-10µs spin_loop, >10µs park.

## Schedule once, reuse across frames

Plan recomputes only on pipeline structure change (new WUs, record
count change, DAG mod). Microsecond cost on const-generic bitmask
structures — but amortised over many frames.

## Self-hosting meta pipeline

The scheduler IS a pipeline. Meta WUs (PlanStage → ScheduleReady →
PassStart → ScheduleEnd) run on the same engine that runs user code.
Meta resources: Dag, ExecutionPlan, LaneAssignment, SchedulerMetrics.
Irreducible kernel ~50 lines.

## Static composition (R6)

All WUs registered at compile time. No runtime registration. Plan
parameters adapt; composition does not.

## Error handling (R7)

`execute() -> ()`. Abort on failure is the default; consumers who
want tolerance wire error columns/virtuals into their pipeline. A
fiber failure freezes that fiber's progress and poisons dependents,
independent fibers continue.

`PipelineResult` reports per-fiber status (Completed / Failed /
Poisoned).

## Columns, not tables

Columns are independent. No tabular joins. `record` is one data
point in a column (not "row", not "entity"). A column has N records;
a morsel windows into a range.

## Deterministic morsel assignment

Not work-stealing by default. Morsels assigned pre-emptively at plan
time. Consumer can override with a stealing Executor trait
implementation for variable per-record workloads.

## Vocabulary discipline

Use canonical terms: pipeline, core, phase, waist, trunk, fiber,
branch, bridge, morsel, micro-morsel, entry, record.

Never use dead terms: chain, chain group, partition, archetype,
entity, row, order.
